preFix.title: Clearing a blocked external drain gully
preFix.subtitle: Unblock a surface-water or yard gully that is not draining
preFix.excerpt: How to identify and clear a blocked external drain gully using a drain rod set or gully grab, flush the gully chamber to remove accumulated silt and debris, and prevent the blockage from recurring with a gully guard.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Thames Water and Anglian Water drainage advice. British Standard BS EN 752 drain and sewer systems.

content.text: A blocked external gully is usually caused by leaf debris and silt accumulating in the

below it. Before using a drain rod, confirm that the blockage is local to the gully: remove the gully grid and look in: if the gully chamber is full of water that is not draining, the blockage is in the trap or the short pipe from gully to the main drain. If the gully is empty, the blockage is further downstream.
Step 1, remove the gully grid and scoop out debris

Lift the gully grid: most are simply resting in a rebate, held by their own weight. With a gloved hand or a trowel, scoop out the accumulated silt and leaf debris from the gully chamber and transfer to a bucket. Work down to the water level in the trap. The bulk removal clears the immediate blockage and makes the rod or plunger easier to use.

Insert the cup plunger into the gully chamber so that the rubber cup seals against the outlet of the trap. Pump the plunger vigorously 8 to 10 times to dislodge the blockage. Watch the water level in the chamber: if the blockage clears, the water will begin to drain away. Remove the plunger and pour two full buckets of water into the gully to flush the cleared debris through.
Step 3, rod the drain if plunging fails
If plunging does not clear the water, the blockage is in the drain pipe beyond the gully trap. Insert the

access on the gully side (if present) or push the first drain rod down through the gully and into the drain pipe. Screw successive rods together as each is inserted, always rotating the rods clockwise to prevent unscrewing. Push firmly until the blockage is felt, then twist and push to break it up. Withdraw the rods carefully without disturbing the joints.
Step 4, flush and clean the gully

Once the drain flows freely, pour 3 to 4 full buckets of water through the gully to flush all dislodged material through to the main drain. Scrub the inside of the gully chamber with an old scrubbing brush to dislodge any remaining deposits. Hose out if a tap is nearby.
Step 5, fit a gully guard and refit the grid

Fit a plastic mesh gully guard inside the gully body, below the grid. The guard catches leaves and large debris before they reach the trap. Clean the guard every autumn. Refit the gully grid and press it firmly into its rebate. Mark the date of clearing: gullies in a high-leaf area should be cleared at least twice a year.

A blocked gully that is part of a combined drain (carrying both surface water and foul water) must be dealt with more carefully: the foul content makes rodding a wet and unpleasant job. If the blockage cannot be cleared with 3 to 4 drain rods, contact a drainage company. Blockages in shared drains (beyond the curtilage of the property) are the responsibility of the water company.

attrs.fix: Trace all pipes connecting to the gully. If another pipe is adding flow that exceeds the drain's capacity, the blockage must be cleared further downstream.
attrs.cause: Downpipe or kitchen waste discharges into the same gully; the blockage is in the shared section downstream.
attrs.symptom: Gully drains but fills again from an unexpected direction

attrs.fix: Fit a finer mesh gully guard and consider cutting back overhanging trees or shrubs that deposit leaves directly into the gully.
attrs.cause: No gully guard fitted; grid openings too large for the debris size.
attrs.symptom: Gully grid constantly blocks with leaves despite regular clearing

attrs.fix: Do not force the rod — it may disconnect and be lost in the drain. Withdraw the rods and call a drainage company with a CCTV camera to inspect the pipe.
attrs.cause: Solid blockage (concrete, collapsed pipe, tree root) rather than silt.
attrs.symptom: Drain rods cannot be pushed past a certain point

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
paragraph reads at grade 11.5 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
